explanation: The phrase "If prop_0, prop_1" is a conditional statement where prop_0 is the condition and prop_1 is the outcome. This translates directly to an implication in LTL: prop_0 implies prop_1.
The natural language statement 'If prop_0, prop_1' indicates a conditional relationship where prop_0 implies prop_1. This translates directly to a logical implication in LTL.
The phrase "If prop_0, prop_1" directly translates to an implication where prop_0 is the antecedent and prop_1 is the consequent. Thus, it means 'prop_0 implies prop_1'.
The phrase "If prop_0, prop_1" directly translates to a logical implication: if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must also be true.

The phrase "If prop_0, prop_1" is interpreted as a universal implication. It means that whenever prop_0 is true at any point in time, prop_1 must also be true at that same point in time. This is captured by the Global (G) operator applied to the implication (->).

The phrase "If prop_0, prop_1" establishes a conditional relationship that is generally understood to hold as a standing rule within a system. This means that at any point in time, if 'prop_0' is true, then 'prop_1' must also be true at that same point. The 'G' (globally) operator ensures that this implication holds true throughout the entire execution of the system.
The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1' directly translates to a logical implication where prop_0 is the antecedent and prop_1 is the consequent. In LTL, this is represented by the implication operator '->'.
The statement "If prop_0, prop_1" is a conditional statement where prop_0 is the antecedent and prop_1 is the consequent. This translates directly to a logical implication: prop_0 implies prop_1.
The statement 'If prop_0, prop_1' translates to a direct logical implication where prop_0 is the condition (antecedent) and prop_1 is the result (consequent).
The phrase "If prop_0, prop_1" is interpreted as a global implication. This means that at every moment in time, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must also be true at that same moment.
